ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general glutinous rice cake entry; no exact entry for red-bean-stuffed steamed variant. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for steamed glutinous rice cake with red bean filling; A0CKM (rice-based sweet snack) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving is approximately one piece (80 g), commonly consumed as a snack or dessert.

nutrient_source.title: 松糕 Steamed Glutinous Rice Cakes Stuffed With Mashed Red Bean — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (cr_id G00010041). All per-100g values are taken as reported with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — glutinous rice / waxy rice reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific steamed glutinous rice cake with red bean filling in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 98 is taken from the published GI value for glutinous (waxy) rice as reported in Atkinson et al. (2021) international GI tables, which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (98 × 20.15) / 100 ≈ 19.7, rounded to 19.6 for an 80 g serving (25.19 g carb per 100 g × 0.80 = 20.15 g carb per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g with partially offsetting potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium content of 231 mg/100 g is in the borderline-moderate range for hypertension management; however, the high potassium content (327 mg/100 g) and dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) are consistent with DASH diet principles and partially offset the sodium load — limit portion to one piece and avoid additional salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic index (~98) from glutinous rice base causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glutinous rice base carries one of the highest GI values of all grains (GI ~98), producing a glycemic load of approximately 19.6 per 80 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food and monitor blood glucose carefully if consumed.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.8 g/100 g), zero trans fat, zero cholesterol, with beneficial dietary fibre and potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and contains no cholesterol or trans fat; the dietary fibre (3.7 g/100 g) and potassium (327 mg/100 g) support cardiovascular health, though the moderate sodium (231 mg/100 g) warrants portion control for those with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Glutinous Rice Cakes Stuffed with Mashed Red Bean (松糕, Song Gao) is a traditional Chinese confection widely enjoyed in Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora. The outer layer is made from glutinous (waxy) rice flour, which is steamed to produce a characteristically soft, chewy, and slightly sticky texture. The filling consists of sweetened red bean paste (azuki beans), which contributes a smooth, creamy, mildly sweet interior that contrasts pleasantly with the tender rice cake exterior. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 25.19 g carbohydrate with 3.7 g dietary fibre from the red bean component. Sodium is 231 mg/100 g and potassium is 327 mg/100 g. The glycemic index is very high (~98) due to the glutinous rice base, making this food unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes unless consumed in very small portions. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is commonly served as a snack or dessert at Chinese festivals, family gatherings, and traditional confectionery shops in Singapore. The steaming preparation method avoids the formation of advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) associated with frying or high-heat dry cooking.
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat red bean rice cake?
How many calories in steamed glutinous rice cake?
What is the GI of glutinous rice?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010041) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from the published glutinous rice GI value in the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables, which is the closest approved analogous source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry. water_g set to -1: moisture content not reported in the HPB source. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: micronutrient data beyond those reported by HPB not available in the HPB Foodo database for this specific dish as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero — steamed plant-based dish with no hydrogenated fats). cholesterol_mg reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source — dish contains no animal-derived fat). saturated_fat_g of 0.8 g/100 g is estimated from the known fat composition of glutinous rice flour and red bean paste components; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI of 98 is an analogous estimate based on glutinous rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in an approved source.

q1_food_identity.notes: Canonical English name present. Chinese (松糕), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi local names all present satisfying G6 Singaporean dish requirements.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 164.0, protein_g 6.5, fat_g 4.21, carbohydrate_g 25.19 all present and sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (exact match, confidence 0.95).

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 3.7, sodium_mg 231.0, saturated_fat_g 0.8, sugar_g 0.21 all present as numeric values supported by HPB source.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 98 (high), gi_category high, glycemic_load_per_serving 19.6, gi_source documented as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn close match to glutinous rice cake URI provided. FoodEx2 code A0CKM used as closest defensible anchor with gap note. SNOMED CT null with honest gap note.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description exceeds 200 characters, covers food identity, preparation method, texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate), Type 2 Diabetes (high), Cardiovascular Disease (low). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name for diabetes correctly set to Type 2 Diabetes.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases array contains all strings from aliases_by_language. Coverage includes English variants, Chinese script, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and romanized forms. Consistency check passed.

Confirmed all three health_context conditions present with correct human_condition_name for Type 2 Diabetes (q7 compliance).
Confirmed multilingual names for Singaporean dish: English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi all present (g6 compliance).
